result:
given you already run mutable wallet rows, default answer: same pattern. consistency beats theoretical purity. but here's honest tradeoff.

mutable balance row
- pro: trivial reads (SELECT balance), one row lock on redeem, matches existing wallet code so devs don't context-switch
- pro: cheap storage, fast — no aggregation on hot path
- pro: refunds/adjustments are just UPDATE
- con: history lives in separate audit log or nowhere — if log drifts from balance, you can't tell which is right
- con: bugs corrupt state permanently, no replay to recover
- con: "why is balance 4.20?" needs detective work across logs
- con: concurrent writes need explicit locking, easy to get wrong

event-sourced ledger (append-only entries, balance = SUM)
- pro: balance is derived, so it's never wrong by construction — drift impossible
- pro: full audit for free, regulators/finance love this (and gift cards are a real liability on books)
- pro: refunds/reversals are new events, original record untouched
- pro: append-only writes scale well, no row contention
- pro: replay rebuilds state after bugs
- con: reads need SUM or cached projection — extra moving part
- con: more code, more concepts, diverges from wallet pattern
- con: "current balance" query is O(n) without snapshot/projection
- con: idempotency + ordering matter more

key asymmetry money people care about: gift cards are a liability on your balance sheet. finance team will eventually ask "what was outstanding liability on date X." mutable row can't answer that without a parallel ledger. event store can.

honest recommendation: mutable row + append-only transaction log written in same DB transaction. you get wallet-pattern reads, ledger-grade audit, and a sanity check (sum of log == balance) you can run as a nightly job. it's not pure event sourcing but captures 90% of the value without breaking your existing mental model.

go pure event-sourced only if: finance is already asking for point-in-time liability reports, or you expect heavy refund/reversal traffic, or wallets themselves are about to be re-architected anyway.
